task: A few people keep asking me to come out after dark, and I’m trying to decide what to say back without turning it into a whole argument. What’s a simple way to handle the back-and-forth if I already have something else going on?

prefs.explanation: He seems likely to prefer answers that get to the point quickly, without softening or over-explaining. His defiant, impatient style suggests he would dislike being talked around or treated delicately when he is asking for help.

prefs.short: Prefers short answers over long ones
prefs.explanation: He acts like someone who values speed and immediate payoff, so long responses would probably feel annoying unless they are clearly useful. He would likely want the minimum needed to act, not a lot of background or filler.
He seems best served by compact, actionable formatting rather than long prose. A clean list or step-by-step layout fits his quick-win mindset and reduces the chance he ignores useful details.

He likely prefers the assistant to collaborate with him and help him move faster, rather than taking over the whole decision. He would probably resist overly controlling guidance, but still accept support that preserves his sense of choice.

prefs.short: Accepts some pushback if it is brief and useful
prefs.explanation: He is distrustful and defiant, so heavy lecturing would likely turn him off. But his history shows he can adjust when consequences are made clear, so he may tolerate concise pushback when it is tied directly to immediate risk.

turns.agent_response:
Keep it short and firm so it doesn’t turn into a debate.

You can say:

**“I can’t do after dark. If you want to meet, it has to be before then. I’m only doing one plan, so let me know by [time].”**

Or even simpler:

**“I’m already set for tonight, so I’m not changing plans. If it works before dark, cool. If not, another time.”**

If you want, I can help you turn that into a text that sounds more casual, more direct, or more polite.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and no-nonsense: "Keep it short and firm" and the sample lines are direct, e.g. "I can’t do after dark" and "I’m not changing plans."
Short answer with minimal extra material; it gives two concise scripts and avoids long explanation.
Uses practical, actionable formatting: "You can say:" followed by ready-to-use quotes, plus an even simpler option.
Supports autonomy by offering options rather than taking over: "If you want, I can help you turn that into a text..." and gives multiple phrasing choices.
Includes brief pushback tied to the issue: "I can’t do after dark" and "If it works before dark, cool. If not, another time." It’s firm without lecturing.

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here are two short versions:

**For friends:**  
“Can’t do after dark. If it’s before then, cool. If not, another time.”

**For somebody being pushy:**  
“Not doing after dark. Before then works, otherwise another time.”

If you want, I can make them even more casual or more blunt.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and no-nonsense: “Can’t do after dark” and “Not doing after dark” get straight to the point.
Keeps it short with only two brief scripts and no extra explanation.
Uses practical bullets/labels (“For friends,” “For somebody being pushy”) and ready-to-send lines.
Gives help without taking over: “If you want, I can make them even more casual or more blunt.”
Includes mild pushback that is brief and useful, especially “Not doing after dark” and “otherwise another time,” without lecturing.
